title: Nonlocal Topological Maxwell Demon Teleporting Ergotropy via Surface-Code Quantum Error Correction
abstract: We introduce a nonlocal Maxwell demon teleporting ergotropy at finite temperature via classical communication and a shared surface code. The teleported ergotropy is exponentially protected below a topological threshold. We identify a thermodynamic phase transition separating a profitable demon phase from a thermal phase. A quadratic infrastructure cost strictly enforces the second law, imposing a fundamental thermodynamic horizon on separation distance. This establishes quantum error correction as a resource for nonlocal thermodynamics beyond fault-tolerant computation.
summary: This paper proposes a method to teleport extractable work across a distance using a shared surface code. It shows that while quantum error correction protects the work from thermal noise, the growing cost of maintaining the error-correcting infrastructure imposes a fundamental limit on how far the work can be sent.
main_problem: Investigating whether ergotropy (extractable work) can be nonlocally transferred at finite temperatures using a Maxwell demon mechanism supported by surface-code quantum error correction.
main_result: The protocol achieves exponential protection of transferred ergotropy below a topological threshold and identifies a thermodynamic horizon (N_max) beyond which the quadratic infrastructure costs make the process unprofitable.
method: The authors use a five-stage protocol involving encoding, measurement, classical communication, decoding (via MWPM), and extraction, supported by Stim and PyMatching simulations.
model_or_system: A rotated surface code on a rectangular lattice with two spatially separated batteries (Alice and Bob) subject to thermal fluctuations and depolarizing noise.
key_observables: Net ergotropy (W_net), success probability (P_succ), entropy production (Delta S_total), and information fraction (f).
important_parameters: Topological threshold (p_th), thermodynamic critical error rate (p_c), separation distance (N), and infrastructure cost scaling (N^2).
main_assumptions: The protocol assumes a causal constraint where syndrome information must travel at a finite signal speed, leading to quadratic scaling of monitoring costs.
figures_summary: Figure 2 shows success probability vs. code distance; a phase diagram maps net ergotropy as a function of error rate and information fraction.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of nonlocal ergotropy transfer, defines the surface code model and protocol, presents numerical results on error thresholds and profitability, and concludes with a thermodynamic analysis of the cost-benefit trade-off.
why_interesting: It bridges quantum error correction with thermodynamics, demonstrating how topological order can be used as a resource for non-local work extraction in the presence of thermal noise.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Five-stage ergotropy teleportation protocol. Purple: Alice operations; teal: Bob operations; blue dashed: logical operator ¯ZL; amber dashed: classical channel; red: thermally excited anyon pairs. Alice charges the logical qubit (stage (1)), measures M = 2L boundary plaquettes (stage (2)), and transmits ⃗sA to Bob (stage (3)). Bob decodes via minimum-weight perfect matching (stage (4)) and conditionally charges his battery (stage (5)), extracting ergotropy EB = (2Psucc −1)∆E with energy balance ∆E + Wops + Wbulk = EB + Qdiss (Eq. 6).

FIG. 2. Topological protection of ergotropy transfer. Decod- ing success probability Psucc versus code distance L at fixed separation N = 310 for four error rates. Below pth ≈0.013 (sub-threshold curves, blue, green, and orange), Psucc →1 ex- ponentially with L, demonstrating that topological protection converts physical qubits directly into recoverable ergotropy. Above threshold (p = 0.015, pink), Psucc →1

FIG. 3. Thermodynamic phase transition at L = 7, N = 40, ∆E = 146.5 J. Stored ergotropy EB = (2Psucc −1)∆E (green dashed), total costs Wops+Wbulk (blue dashed), and net work Wnet (red solid) as functions of the physical error rate p. The demon phase (Wnet > 0, green shading) and thermal phase (Wnet < 0, red shading) are separated by the thermodynamic critical error rate pc ≈0.014 (vertical dotted line), which ex- ceeds the topological threshold pth ≈0.013. The two phase boundaries are physically distinct. The transition is contin- uous, reflecting the smooth degradation of Psucc(p) with in- creasing temperature. Each point: 8000 Monte Carlo shots.

FIG. 4. Ergotropy versus syndrome rounds at L = 7, N = 40, p = 0.005. Without error correction (gray dashed), the er- gotropy decays exponentially on a timescale independent of N, reflecting the entropic instability of two-dimensional topo- logical order at any T > 0 [17]. With active monitoring (or- ange), the demon sustains EB near ∆E over 80 rounds. The shaded area quantifies the ergotropy recovered by continuous operation. It represents the thermodynamic value of the in- formation extracted per syndrome round. Each point: 8000 shots.
